sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Helmet removal may be necessary when the airway cannot be assessed or managed adequately with the helmet in place. If the airway is stable and the helmet is not obstructing care, removal may be unnecessary and can create avoidable spinal movement.

sections.title: Two-Person Technique
sections.content:
**Consider removal for:** airway compromise, vomiting, respiratory arrest, cardiac arrest, or inability to assess and manage the patient adequately

1. Use a coordinated two-person technique with one rescuer dedicated to head and neck control

2. Open or cut the chin strap and prepare for the helmet to catch on the nose or occiput as it comes off

3. Remove the helmet slowly while maintaining manual support of the head and neck throughout

4. Once removed, immediately reassess airway access, head position, and need for padding or further spinal motion restriction

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Good communication between rescuers matters more than speed
- After removal, the occiput may need padding to maintain neutral alignment
- Airway access and ventilation take priority if the patient is deteriorating
- Document why the helmet was removed and any immediate findings or complications
